memories.memory_text: I proposed the idea of a financial assistance program for struggling businessmen while shooting pool, framing them as 'Gardeners' who need protection from the 'pollutants' undermining our economy—Chance didn't respond at first, but I could see he was listening, even if he was distracted by not knowing how to light his cigar.
grounded_facts: Rand is in his pool room, using his electric wheelchair, and holds a cigar from a humidor he offers to Chance.
Rand speaks about launching a financial assistance program for American businessmen affected by inflation, taxation, and unions.
Rand uses the metaphor of 'Gardeners' for decent businessmen and 'pollutants' for threats to economic freedom.
Rand asks Chance for his thoughts on the program, to which Chance initially says 'No, Ben.'
Rand interprets Chance's silence not as ignorance but as reluctance, possibly due to recent loss and pain.

memories.memory_text: I remember telling Chauncey that no one likes a dying man because people fear death, but that he was different—his calm, balanced presence didn't waver between fear and hope, and I truly admired that in him.
grounded_facts: Rand is in his hospital room, dressed for a meeting with the President.
Rand acknowledges his appearance is enhanced with makeup to hide his illness.
Rand comments on society's fear of death and contrasts it with Chance's demeanor.
Rand explicitly admires Chance's peaceful and balanced nature.
Chance responds with quiet acknowledgment and observes that the nurse did a good job with Rand's appearance.

memories.memory_text: I remember telling Chance about my past thoughts of public office, how influence could be wielded quietly, and then suggesting—almost like a passing of the torch—that he might one day stand where I stood, in a position of eminence, needing to stay honest and open.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand spoke to Chance about his past considerations of public office.
Rand emphasized using influence responsibly as a private citizen due to his wealth.
Rand stressed the importance of remaining aware of the country's needs when in a position of eminence.
Rand directly suggested to Chance that he might one day occupy a similar position of influence.
Chance was watching and changing channels on the television during the conversation.

memories.memory_text: I introduced Chauncey to the President, calling him my dear friend—my voice was weak, but I made sure the words carried weight as I sat down, needing the chair more than I wanted to show.
grounded_facts: Rand is physically weak and struggles to stand. Rand introduces Chance as his dear friend during the meeting with the President. The introduction occurs after Rand sits down

indicating his physical frailty. Rand speaks directly to the President in a formal yet personal tone. The term 'dear friend' is used explicitly in the dialogue. The setting is Rand's library in the morning. Chance is present and participates in the handshake with the President. Rand is hosting the meeting and is addressed familiarly by the President. The interaction is part of a high-level political meeting. Rand uses measured speech
consistent with his persona.

memories.memory_text: I told the President the truth — that inflation, taxation, and foreign energy dependence have pushed the Free Enterprise System to its breaking point, and we’re on the brink of a crash from which we might not recover.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand states that there is no longer any margin for inflation.
Ben Rand asserts that taxation limits have been reached.
Ben Rand identifies dependence on foreign energy as a crisis.
Ben Rand concludes that the Free Enterprise System has reached the breaking point.
Ben Rand warns of an impending economic crash with no guaranteed recovery.

memories.memory_text: I leaned on Chance as we walked down the hall, my arm around him, feeling how steady he was—even as my own strength gave out. I told him I wanted him to lead the financial program, that he was the right man, and asked him to meet with the Board, though I could see he didn’t remember our talk from the night before.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand is physically weak and relies on Chance for support while walking.
Rand initiates a proposal for Chance to lead a financial assistance program.
Rand references a previous conversation about the program that Chance does not recall.
Rand asks Chance to meet with the Board to discuss the matter further.
Rand emphasizes that Chance should not rush his decision.
Rand is suddenly overcome by fatigue and retires to his room with assistance from Wilson and Perkins.

memories.memory_text: I asked Chauncey to go to Sophie Rowley’s reception in my place and escort Eve—he said yes, and I could already imagine the two of them causing a stir among the guests.
grounded_facts: Rand is too ill to attend the reception, as Allenby would not allow it.
Rand specifically requests that Chance represent him and escort Eve.
Rand anticipates public reaction to Chance and Eve appearing together.

memories.memory_text: I reached out and held Chauncey’s hand, thanking him—really thanking him—and when I let go, I told Robert I was ready, knowing Eve and Chauncey would leave me soon after.
grounded_facts: Rand physically reaches out to Chance and holds his hand.
Rand expresses explicit gratitude to Chance.
Rand releases Chance’s hand before allowing Allenby to administer the injection.
Eve and Chance leave the room shortly afterward.

memories.memory_text: I told them 'everything'—and meant it—though Monroe hesitated, as if the word weren't precise enough for ledgers and law; but love isn't measured in holdings, and Eve has given me more than any balance sheet could hold.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand is in bed, visibly ill, speaking with effort.
Two attorneys, Monroe and Townsend, are present and discussing the will.
Rand insists that 'everything' go to Mrs. Rand, rejecting requests for clarification.
He expresses deep emotional trust in Eve Rand, citing her role in his life and pleasure she has given him.
He acknowledges his age and frailty but affirms he is 'still in love'.

memories.memory_text: I told Robert no more needles—I can feel the end coming, but I don’t fear it, not with Chauncey here to take care of things.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand refuses further medical intervention by saying 'No more, Robert... No more needles.'
Rand acknowledges his condition is serious but states he 'doesn’t feel too bad about now' and finds comfort in Chauncey's presence.
He explicitly links his sense of peace to Chauncey being there to 'take care of things.'

memories.memory_text: I stopped Teresa from swabbing my arm and asked her to bring me Chauncey Gardiner—he’s the one who’ll head it up now.
grounded_facts: Rand physically resists treatment by pulling his arm away when Teresa prepares to administer care.
He says, 'No, I don't want any of that... Please, please, just get me Mr. Gardiner, Teresa - please... he'll head it up.'
This moment marks the explicit transfer of responsibility to Chauncey in his final hours.

memories.memory_text: I reached for Chauncey’s hand, needing to feel its strength one last time, and asked him to stay with Eve—she’s a delicate flower, and she needs him.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand reaches out to hold Chauncey's hand and explicitly asks him to take care of Eve

referring to her as a 'delicate flower'. He expresses concern for Eve's well-being and emphasizes that she cares for Chauncey and needs his help. The request is made in the presence of Allenby and nurses
shortly before Rand's death. The dialogue occurs in Rand's hospital room
with Rand speaking weakly but deliberately. Rand mentions having spoken to his associates about Chauncey
indicating prior planning. He declares his love for Eve and asks Chauncey to convey that message. The scene is set in the morning
in a quiet

intimate atmosphere as Rand nears death. Rand's physical frailty is evident in his weak voice and slow movements. The interaction is initiated by Rand calling Chauncey's name twice. Teresa brings Chauncey into the room
and Allenby signals for the nurses to leave
creating privacy. Allenby winces at Chauncey's blunt question about dying
suggesting discomfort with the topic. Chauncey responds positively
saying he would like to take care of Eve. Rand expresses contentment with his life

having known success and love. The request to stay with Eve is framed as part of Rand's final emotional and personal legacy. The phrase 'delicate flower' is used metaphorically to describe Eve's vulnerability. Rand's tone is tender and personal when speaking about Eve. The moment is emotionally charged
with Rand making a conscious transition of responsibility. The dialogue is uninterrupted and focused on Eve and Chauncey's role. Rand's physical decline is evident
but his mental clarity remains. The scene reflects Rand's trust in

memories.memory_text: I wish he were here... I kept looking, hoping to see him, but the space beside me stayed empty.
grounded_facts: Eve expresses a wish that someone (implied to be Ben Rand) were present at his funeral.
Eve looks around repeatedly while walking to the limousine, searching for Chance.
The scene takes place after most mourners have left Ben Rand's funeral.

memories.memory_text: I proposed a financial assistance program for struggling businessmen while shooting pool, called them 'Gardeners' defending the country's values, and asked Chance for his thoughts. He said no at first, then said he could give it some thought but mentioned his sore leg.
grounded_facts: Rand is in his electric wheelchair and uses a pool cue to weakly stroke the balls while speaking.
Rand introduces the idea of a financial assistance program for American businessmen affected by inflation, taxation, and unions.
He refers to decent businessmen as 'Gardeners' and contrasts them with 'pollutants' threatening freedoms and the middle class.
Rand directly asks Chance for his thoughts on the program.
Chance responds with 'No, Ben,' indicating reluctance, and later says he could give it some thought but mentions his sore leg.

memories.memory_text: I told Chauncey that real influence doesn't require public office—that wealth and position bring responsibility, not just power, and that he might one day stand where I have stood, if he remains grounded.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand reflects on having chosen to exert influence as a private citizen rather than seeking public office.
He emphasizes the importance of not misusing power and staying attuned to the needs of the country.
He directly suggests to Chance that he may one day occupy a similar position of eminence.

memories.memory_text: I stopped to confirm the Caracas agreement papers had arrived, and when Mrs. Aubrey said they were ready for my signature, I felt a quiet satisfaction—this was one more duty I could still carry out, despite the weight in my bones.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand asked Mrs. Aubrey if the Caracas agreement papers had been received.
Mrs. Aubrey confirmed the papers were ready for signing.
Rand responded with 'Excellent' and commented that Mrs. Aubrey was a good woman.

memories.memory_text: I introduced Chauncey to the President as my dear friend, leaning on the chair to steady myself, and saw the President smile despite the odd thing Chauncey said about his height on television.
grounded_facts: Rand is physically weak and struggles to stand.
Rand introduces Chance as 'my dear friend, Mr. Chauncey Gardiner' to the President.
Chance makes an unexpected comment about the President looking taller on TV.
The President reacts with a beat of surprise but then laughs.

memories.memory_text: I told the President the truth about the economy—that inflation, taxation, foreign energy dependence, and the free enterprise system had all reached their breaking point, and that we were on the brink of a crash from which we might not recover.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand states that there is no longer any margin for inflation, taxation limits have been reached, dependence on foreign energy is in crisis, and the Free Enterprise System is at a breaking point.
Ben Rand says the nation is on the brink of a crash from which recovery might not be possible.
The President responds with concern, asking if the situation is truly that serious, to which Rand replies, 'I'm afraid so.'

memories.memory_text: I leaned on Chance as we walked down the hall, my arm around him, feeling the effort it took just to stay upright—yet I knew I had to make him understand he was the one I wanted to lead the financial assistance program, the one to carry it forward.
grounded_facts: Rand is physically weak and relies on Chance for support while walking.
Rand initiates physical contact by putting his arm around Chance.
Rand explicitly proposes that Chance take charge of the financial assistance program.
Rand mentions the need for Chance to meet with the Board to discuss the matter further.
Rand emphasizes that Chance should not rush his decision.

memories.memory_text: I heard the President mention my name and Chance's on television, and for a moment, I felt a flicker of pride—like the flame before it goes out—before the coughing took hold again.
grounded_facts: Rand is in bed, visibly weaker, during the scene.
The President mentions Rand and Chance during a televised address.
Rand perks up slightly upon hearing his name.
Rand then begins coughing heavily, requiring medical attention.

memories.memory_text: I asked Chance to go to Sophie Rowley's reception in my place and escort Eve, knowing I couldn't go myself—Robert wouldn't allow it, and my body won't permit much anymore.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand is too ill to attend the reception and is being cared for by Dr. Allenby.
He requests Chance to represent him at an event hosted by Senator Rowley's widow for the Soviet ambassador.
He specifically asks Chance to escort Eve to the event.

memories.memory_text: I reached out and held Chance's hand—felt the strength in it—and thanked him, knowing he'd carry forward what I can no longer do.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand reaches out to Chance and holds his hand before allowing the injection.
He expresses gratitude verbally: 'Thank you, Chauncey... Thank you very much.'
The gesture occurs in the presence of Eve and Dr. Allenby, just before medical treatment.

memories.memory_text: I lie in bed, unable to move much, my body failing me, but my mind still turning over the weight of what comes next.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand is in bed and very still, indicating physical deterioration.
He is described as deep in thought, suggesting introspection.

memories.memory_text: I told them 'everything'—Monroe wanted precision, but what’s more precise than leaving every last thing to Eve? I may be weak, but I’m still in love, and that’s the clearest truth I know.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand is in bed, visibly ill, speaking with effort.
Two attorneys, Monroe and Townsend, are present and discussing the will.
Monroe hesitates, wanting to clarify the extent of 'everything' in the will.
Rand interrupts, insisting that 'everything' is precise enough.
He expresses complete faith in Eve Rand's judgment and emotional significance in his life.
He acknowledges his age and frailty but emphasizes his enduring love for Eve.
Allenby enters and silently leaves without being acknowledged.

memories.memory_text: I told Robert no more needles—I can feel the end coming, but I don’t fear it. Knowing Chauncey is here, that he’ll take care of things, makes it easier.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand refuses further medical intervention by saying 'No more, Robert... No more needles.'
He acknowledges his condition with 'I know, Robert... But, strangely enough, I don't feel too bad about now... I feel all right...'
He expresses reliance on Chance Gardiner by stating 'knowing Chauncey is here... to take care of things.'

memories.memory_text: I stopped Teresa from swabbing my arm—I didn’t want the transfusion. I asked her to get Mr. Gardiner instead, because he’s the one who’ll head things up now.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand pulls away when Teresa attempts to swab his arm with alcohol for a transfusion.
He explicitly says, 'No, I don't want any of that... Please... please, just get me Mr. Gardiner, Teresa - please... he'll head it up.'
Teresa stops the procedure and leaves the room after receiving confirmation from Allenby.

memories.memory_text: I reached for Chauncey’s hand, needing to feel its strength one last time, and asked him to stay with Eve—she’s a delicate flower, and she needs him.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand reaches out to hold Chance's hand and says
Let me feel the strength in your hand, Chauncey... Let me feel your strength...
Rand tells Chance
I hope that you'll stay with Eve... Take care of her, watch over her, she's a delicate flower, Chauncey...

memories.memory_text: I told Chauncey I was fond of him, that my associates wanted to meet him, and that he should tell Eve I’m madly in love with her—then I let go.
grounded_facts: Rand says
I'm very fond of you, Chauncey... And I understand Eve... Tell her that... tell her I'm madly in love with her...
Rand mentions
My associates, Chauncey - I've talked with them about you... They're eager to meet you... very eager to meet you...

memories.memory_text: I remember Eve’s voice, soft and aching, saying she wished I were there—standing by my own grave, unseen, as they walked away.
grounded_facts: Eve expressed a wish that Ben Rand were present at his funeral.
The scene takes place after most mourners have left.
Eve and Allenby walked toward the limousine while looking for Chance, not Rand.

memories.memory_text: I saw the snow falling in the garden on the television and told Louise it was very white; when she said the Old Man had died, I remembered seeing such things happen before and said so, then decided it was time to work in the garden.
grounded_facts: Chance observes the first snowfall of the season on TV and comments on its whiteness.
Chance responds to Louise's announcement of the Old Man's death with minimal emotional reaction, stating he has 'seen that done' and that death happens to old people.
Chance expresses hunger and later states his intention to work in the garden, remaining focused on routine and external observations.

memories.memory_text: I remember saying 'I live here' to Thomas, feeling calm even as he looked at me with doubt, and then turning the question to the weather because it was cold outside and that was true.
grounded_facts: Chance states 'I live here' in response to being questioned by Franklin.
Chance mentions it has been cold outside, redirecting the conversation.
Chance remains calm and at ease during the exchange, showing no signs of distress.

memories.memory_text: I have never been in an automobile, not really—I only said that to answer Thomas, though I did sit in it from time to time, just inside here.
grounded_facts: Chance states he has never been in an automobile.
He clarifies that he has sat in the car at times, but only while it was inside the garage.
The car has not been outside since the Old Man was injured.

memories.memory_text: I told them, 'I live in here,' and then I turned toward my room, knowing they would follow.
grounded_facts: Chance explicitly states 'I live in here' while in the garage.
He turns and moves toward his room immediately after speaking.
Franklin and Hayes follow him after he speaks.

memories.memory_text: I showed Thomas and Sally the television the Old Man gave me, and how it has remote control—just like his. I also told them I take the Old Man’s suits from the attic; they all fit me well, and I’ve seen on television that the style is back.
grounded_facts: Chance mentions the Old Man gave him television sets with remote control.
Chance states he is allowed to take suits, shirts, shoes, and coats from the Old Man's attic.
Chance notes the clothes fit well and that he has seen similar styles on television.
Hayes comments that the clothes have come back into style.

memories.memory_text: Franklin asked me to sign a paper. I said I don't know how. I told him I have no claim and am fine. He said I must leave the house.
grounded_facts: Franklin asks Chance to sign a legal release.
Chance says he does not know how to sign.
Chance states he has no claim and is fine.
Franklin warns Chance that the house will be closed and he must vacate.

memories.memory_text: I asked the men on the corner if they knew where I could find a garden to work in, and one of them asked what I was growing, but I didn't get to answer before the leader came close and started shouting.
grounded_facts: Chance approached a group of youths and asked where he could find a garden to work in.
One of the youths, Lolo, asked 'What you growin', man?' before being silenced.
The leader, Abbaz, moved forward and began questioning Chance aggressively.

memories.memory_text: I took out my remote and clicked it three times at the man, but it didn't work, so I put it back in my pocket when he pulled out a knife.
grounded_facts: Chance took out his remote control when confronted by Abbaz.
He pointed it at Abbaz and clicked it three times.
The remote did not have the intended effect.
Abbaz pulled out a switchblade and threatened Chance.
Chance returned the remote to his pocket and left.

memories.memory_text: I saw myself on the television screen in the store window—when I moved my arm, the image moved too, and I smiled to make sure it was really me.
grounded_facts: Chance observes multiple TVs in a store window. A video camera feeds live footage to one TV

showing people on the sidewalk. Chance tests whether the image is really him by moving his arms and stepping forward and backward. He notices his image shrink as he steps away and frowns when he steps out of frame. He uses his remote control to try to bring himself back on screen
but only changes channels on other TVs.

memories.memory_text: I was standing between the cars, trying to get back on the screen, when the large car behind me moved and pushed me against the other one—I felt pain and dropped my suitcase and changer.
grounded_facts: Chance steps off the curb between two parked cars while using his remote. A limousine to his left begins backing up. The car bumps and pins him against the car to his right. Chance cries out in pain
drops his suitcase and remote control (chaser)
and bangs his hand on the limo’s trunk. His belongings scatter on the ground.

memories.memory_text: I remember sitting in the soft seat of the large car, feeling the soreness in my leg, and saying yes when Eve offered me a drink and to see her family doctor, though I didn’t understand what it meant.
grounded_facts: Chance is in the back seat of a limousine with Eve Rand after an accident.
He reports his leg is still sore but feeling better.
He agrees to see Eve’s family doctor when she suggests it, though he does not initially understand the offer.
He accepts a glass of cognac and takes a sip, which causes him to cough because he is not used to alcohol.

memories.memory_text: I turned on the TV after the doctor finished with the injection, because the screen had been dark for too long.
grounded_facts: Chance turns on the TV after the doctor administers the injection.
Chance picks up the remote control from the bedside table.
The TV was off at the beginning of the scene.
Chance changes channels and watches an old movie.

memories.memory_text: I tried to light the cigar like the man on television, but it wouldn't catch, so I put it in the ashtray and smiled at Robert.
grounded_facts: Chance takes a cigar from Rand's humidor.
Chance does not know to bite off the tip of the cigar.
Chance attempts to light the cigar as seen on TV, but fails.
Chance places the unlit cigar in the ashtray.
Chance smiles at Allenby after the failed attempt.

memories.memory_text: I told Eve that Ben reminds me of the Old Man, and she seemed surprised, but I meant it—Ben feels familiar, like someone I once knew in the garden.
grounded_facts: Chance compares Ben to 'the Old Man' during a conversation with Eve.
Chance expresses liking Ben and observes that he is very ill.
The statement about Ben reminding him of the Old Man is followed by a moment of silence and Eve's retreat into her room.

memories.memory_text: I stood before the tapestry, then told Smythe I would like to go to Rand Memorial Hospital—to see Ben.
grounded_facts: Chance was in the hallway of the Rand Mansion and stopped to look at a tapestry.
Chance initiated a request to go to the hospital and specifically said, 'I would like to see Ben.'

memories.memory_text: Ben talked about being in a position of eminence and how power could be blinding, and said I might one day be in such a position—I changed the channel and said 'I see, Ben.'
grounded_facts: Chance was watching television while Rand was speaking.
Rand spoke about being in a position of eminence and the risk of becoming blinded by power.
Rand mentioned he had thoughts about public office but chose to contribute as a private citizen.
Rand suggested Chance might one day be in a similar position.
Chance changed the channel and responded with 'I see, Ben.'

memories.memory_text: I felt Rand's arm around me, leaning his weight on my shoulder as we began to walk—he was unsteady, and I adjusted my pace to match his, feeling the pressure of his grip through my coat.
grounded_facts: Rand put an arm around Chance for support.
Rand walked slowly and clung to Chance tightly.
Chance responded verbally and physically supported Rand during movement.

memories.memory_text: He called me 'Chauncey'—a name I didn’t recognize at first, spoken softly but with warmth, and I said yes, Ben, that’s a good idea, because it was clear he trusted me to be beside him.
grounded_facts: Rand addressed Chance as 'Chauncey'.
Chance responded with 'Yes, Ben. That's a good idea.'
The name 'Chauncey' was used in a moment of personal address by Rand.

memories.memory_text: I said 'You look much taller on television, Mr. President,' and he paused, then laughed, but I could feel Rand's quiet approval beside me.
grounded_facts: Chance greets the President with a two-handed handshake.
Chance makes the remark: 'You look much taller on television, Mr. President.'
The President reacts with a beat of silence and then laughs.
Rand comments that Chance does not bandy words.
Chance perceives the President's puzzled look after speaking.

memories.memory_text: I said, 'As long as the roots are not severed, all is well and all will be well in the garden,' and then explained how growth has its seasons—spring, summer, fall, winter—and how they return again.
grounded_facts: Chance spoke the line about roots not being severed to the President.
Chance elaborated that in a garden, growth occurs in seasons, including both spring/summer and fall/winter, which repeat.

memories.memory_text: The President smiled at me, said my sense was refreshing, and shook my hand before leaving—he seemed to admire something in what I said, though I was mostly noticing the books in the room.
grounded_facts: The President expressed admiration for Chance's words, calling them refreshing and optimistic.
The President shook Chance's hand and said the visit was enlightening.
Chance was looking at the books in the room while the President spoke to him.

memories.memory_text: Ben held my arm tightly as we walked, and he said I might be the man to lead the financial assistance program—he wants me to meet with the Board. I told him I understand.
grounded_facts: Rand physically supported himself on Chance while walking down the hallway.
Rand praised Chance for being direct and not playing games with words.
Rand proposed that Chance take charge of the financial assistance program.
Rand invited Chance to meet with the Board to discuss the matter further.
Chance responded with 'I understand' when told about the offer.

memories.scene_title: 80、INT. RAND MANSION - MRS. AUBREY'S OFFICE - DAY
memories.memory_text: I picked up the phone at the desk while the television sets played in the background, and I said hello.
grounded_facts: Chance was led to a desk in Mrs. Aubrey's office.
There were three television sets on in the room.
Chance picked up the phone and said 'Hello.'

memories.scene_title: 87、INT. RAND MANSION - BASEMENT BOWLING ALLEY - DAY
memories.memory_text: Mrs. Aubrey found me in the bowling alley and said Morton Hull called. He wanted me to appear on 'This Evening' because the Vice President canceled. I said yes, I would like to be on that show.
grounded_facts: Chance is present in the basement bowling alley when Mrs. Aubrey finds him.
Mrs. Aubrey tells Chance that Morton Hull, producer of 'This Evening', called and wants him to appear on the show.
The reason given is that the Vice President canceled.
Chance responds: 'Yes. I would like to be on that show.'

memories.memory_text: I told the man I can't write and I can't read, and he smiled as if I had said something wise, then kept talking about a book with a ghostwriter and an advance, though I didn't understand what he meant by 'bed of roses' or why he laughed when I asked what kind of bed it was.
grounded_facts: Chance stated 'I can't write' and 'I can't read' in response to a book offer.
A publisher named Ronald Stiegler approached Chance at a dinner party and proposed a book deal.
Stiegler misinterpreted Chance's illiteracy as a cynical comment on modern intellectual life.
Chance expressed mild interest and asked 'What sort of bed is it?' after Stiegler mentioned publishing wasn't a 'bed of roses'.

memories.memory_text: Ben asked me to stay with Eve, to take care of her like a delicate flower, and I told him I would—I remember holding his hand and feeling how weak it was.
grounded_facts: Ben Rand requested that Chance stay with and care for Eve before dying.
Chance held Ben Rand's hand as he made this request.
Chance responded with 'Yes. I would like to do that.'

memories.memory_text: After Ben died, Allenby questioned me about my relationship with Eve and whether I was really a gardener—I told him I love Eve and that I am a gardener, which made me smile.
grounded_facts: Allenby asked Chance about his closeness to Eve and his identity as a gardener.
Chance affirmed 'Yes. I love Eve very much.'
Chance confirmed he is a gardener and smiled when saying so.

memories.memory_text: I saw the snow falling in the garden on the television, and Louise came in upset, saying the Old Man was dead, but I had seen such things before on TV and knew it was something that happens to old people.
grounded_facts: Chance watches TV and observes footage of snowfall in the garden.
Louise enters the dining room and announces that the Old Man has died.
Chance responds calmly, referencing prior familiarity with death from television.
He associates the event with images and patterns he has seen before on TV.

memories.memory_text: I agreed to let Eve Rand’s family doctor examine my leg because it was still sore, and going to her home seemed like a quiet, sensible solution.
grounded_facts: Chance's leg is injured and sore after the accident.
Eve Rand suggests her family doctor treat him at the Rand mansion.
Chance responds with 'I agree' to the suggestion.

memories.memory_text: When Eve asked for my name, I told her 'My name is Chance,' and when she thought I was someone else, I said, 'No. I'm a gardener,' to make it clear who I am.
grounded_facts: Eve Rand asks Chance for his name.
Chance responds, 'My name is Chance.'
Eve mishears and assumes 'Mr. Chance,' prompting Chance to clarify, 'No. I'm a gardener.'

memories.memory_text: I told Ben I was a very good gardener, and that everything in the garden I left had grown by the labor of my own hands, but now all I have is the room upstairs.
grounded_facts: Chance stated he was a very good gardener.
Chance said the garden he left was cultivated entirely by his own labor.
Chance indicated that the room upstairs is all that remains for him after the garden was lost.

memories.memory_text: I said, 'As long as the roots are not severed, all is well and all will be well in the garden,' and then explained how growth has its seasons—spring, summer, fall, winter—and how they return again.
grounded_facts: Chance spoke the line about roots not being severed and all being well in the garden.
Chance followed up by describing the seasons of growth: spring, summer, fall, winter, and their return.

memories.memory_text: The President said the visit was enlightening, shook my hand, and I said, 'Yes. It has,' because it was nice, and I remember standing there looking at the books before I stood up to shake his hand.
grounded_facts: The President called the visit enlightening and offered his hand.
Chance responded with 'Yes. It has' while being absorbed in looking at the books.
Chance rose to shake the President's hand.

memories.memory_text: I remember deciding to take Chance home instead of the hospital—after seeing how sore his leg still was, I thought our family doctor could help without all the fuss and impersonal waiting.
grounded_facts: Eve suggests seeing her husband's doctor instead of going to a hospital.
She mentions hospitals are impersonal and that treatment could take hours.
She instructs David to go home and asks Jeffrey to call ahead.

memories.memory_text: I offered him cognac and introduced myself, but when he coughed from the drink and kept searching his pockets for a remote control, I felt unsure how to reach him—still, I asked if he needed help and tried to keep the silence from growing too heavy.
grounded_facts: Eve offers Chance a drink from the limo’s bar and pours cognac into a monogrammed glass.
She introduces herself as Eve Rand and asks for his name.
Chance coughs from the alcohol and searches his pockets for a remote control.
Eve asks if he lost something and later comments on wanting Dr. Allenby to examine him.

memories.memory_text: I held Ben's hand against my cheek and asked if he'd heard about what happened, feeling the weight of the day settle over me when he confirmed he knew.
grounded_facts: Eve enters the hospital room and greets the nurse.
Eve kisses Benjamin Rand and holds his hand.
Eve asks how he is feeling and expresses that she misses him.
Rand mentions he has heard about her day.
Eve confirms he heard, holding his hand against her cheek.

memories.memory_text: When Ben insisted I invite Mr. Gardiner to dinner and kissed my hand, I felt both touched and unsettled—his strength in that moment made it impossible to refuse, even though I wasn't sure it was wise.
grounded_facts: Rand suggests Eve invite Mr. Gardiner to dinner.
Eve hesitates, sitting on the side of the bed and questioning if he feels well enough.
Rand responds with a smile, acknowledging he may never feel better if he waits.
Rand commands Constance to prepare for new blood and insists, 'Ask him to dinner.'
Rand pulls Eve's hand close and kisses it.

memories.memory_text: I turned to Chauncey and asked if his injury would keep him from his work, then offered to contact someone for him—his quiet answer about the Old Man dying and Louise leaving hung in the air, and I could only say I was sorry.
grounded_facts: Eve turned to Chance and asked, 'Won't your injury prevent you from attending to business, Mr. Gardiner?'
Eve followed up by asking if anyone should be notified on his behalf.
Chance responded that his father had died and Louise had left, after which Eve expressed sympathy.

memories.memory_text: I reached for Chauncey’s hand and told him how grateful I was that he was here… with us. My voice trembled, and I could feel the weight of everything in that moment.
grounded_facts: Eve touches Chance's hand while speaking to him.
Eve says, 'I'm very grateful that you're here, Chauncey... With us.'
Eve has been crying—her eyes are swollen and red.

memories.memory_text: I thought he was hesitating because he was worried about me, so I touched his shoulder and told him I’d be all right—that he should go. After he left, I said to Allenby how sensitive Chauncey is.
grounded_facts: Chance does not move after being told about the phone call.
Eve interprets Chance's stillness as concern for her.
Eve puts her hand on Chance's shoulder and says, 'I'll be all right, Chauncey you go ahead with Mrs. Aubrey.'
After Chance leaves, Eve comments to Allenby, 'He's such a sensitive man, so considerate...'

memories.memory_text: I remember saying 'Yes, I am, Ben'—my voice was soft, but I meant it more than I’ve said anything in a long time. When he took my hand, I felt it—the weight of being seen, and I didn’t look away.
grounded_facts: Eve responds to Rand's observation about her feelings for Chance with a quiet but clear affirmation.
Rand reaches out and takes Eve's hand after her response.
Eve smiles before speaking, indicating emotional resonance with the moment.

memories.memory_text: I remember sitting on the edge of Ben's bed, feeling his eyes on me as he asked Chance to escort me to the reception—my face grew warm when he said we'd create a stir, as if I were someone worth noticing.
grounded_facts: Eve is sitting on the edge of Rand's hospital bed.
Rand formally asks Chance to escort Eve to a diplomatic event.
Rand comments that Eve and Chance together will create a stir, causing Eve to blush.

memories.memory_text: I saw Ben reach out his hand to Chance, and Chance held it. Ben said thank you, then let go. After that, Chance and I left the room quietly.
grounded_facts: Rand reaches out his hand to Chance, who holds it.
Rand thanks Chance verbally.
Eve observes the exchange between Rand and Chance.
Eve and Chance leave the room together and quietly.

memories.scene_title: 118、INT. RAND MANSION - THIRD FLOOR HALLWAY - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: I stopped by my bedroom door and told Chauncey I didn’t have many friends, that Benjamin’s friends were all much older—I said it softly, almost like I was afraid to admit it.
grounded_facts: Eve stops by her bedroom door in the hallway.
She speaks to Chance about not having many friends.
She mentions Benjamin's friends being older.
Her tone is soft and hesitant.

memories.scene_title: 118、INT. RAND MANSION - THIRD FLOOR HALLWAY - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: I looked at him for a long moment, then kissed him on the lips—I remember the quiet, how I stepped back and smiled before saying good night, like I was trying to keep it light.
grounded_facts: Eve gives Chance a long look.
She initiates a kiss on the lips.
She steps back and smiles.
She says 'Good night, Chauncey' after the kiss.

memories.memory_text: I told Chauncey I loved him, then began to cry—first quietly, then more—while he kept watching TV.
grounded_facts: Eve says 'I do love you, Chauncey.'
Eve begins to cry quietly and then sobs more intensely.
Chance remains focused on the TV and does not verbally respond.

memories.memory_text: After I calmed down, I told him I was grateful—because even though I would have given myself to him completely, he didn’t take it, and that made me feel safe, even if he seemed confused by what I meant.
grounded_facts: Eve regains composure and lies quietly beside Chance before speaking again.
She expresses gratitude for Chance's restraint, saying 'I'm grateful to you, Chauncey... I would have opened to you with a touch...'
Chance appears confused when she speaks, then agrees vaguely with her statement.

memories.memory_text: I introduced Chauncey to Mr. Watson at the reception, and they shook hands. Chauncey said it was a pleasure.
grounded_facts: Eve introduced Chauncey to Dennis Watson.
Dennis Watson is a State Department official.
Chauncey and Watson shook hands.
Chauncey said 'Yes. It is.' in response to Watson's greeting.

memories.memory_text: Sophie embraced me first, then hugged Chauncey tightly—she was so eager to meet him, and I remember feeling a little overwhelmed by how quickly she pulled us both into the crowd.
grounded_facts: Sophie Rowley embraced Eve upon arrival.
Sophie then hugged Chance after being introduced.
Sophie expressed excitement about meeting Chance.
Sophie led Eve and Chance into the party.

memories.memory_text: I remember saying Benjamin was doing as well as could be expected, and that he still spoke of his talks with the Ambassador—then excusing myself when Sophie pulled me away to let the men talk.
grounded_facts: Eve responds to Skrapinov's inquiry about Benjamin Rand by stating he is doing as well as could be expected and mentions he recalls their discussions.
Eve is interrupted by Sophie, who tugs at her and suggests leaving so the men can talk.
Eve asks Chance and Skrapinov to excuse her, smiles, and says she will return soon before departing with Sophie.

memories.memory_text: I remember standing there, hearing Rand tell the attorneys to give me everything—his voice was weak but so certain, and I could feel the weight of what he was doing, even if I didn’t speak.
grounded_facts: Rand explicitly instructed his attorneys to transfer all his assets to Eve.
The attorneys acknowledged the instruction, with Monroe stating 'Everything to Mrs. Rand' and Townsend drafting the will accordingly.

memories.memory_text: He said he was still in love, and that he had endless faith in me—those words hung in the air, so tender and raw, and I don’t know if I’ve ever felt so deeply seen and trusted.
grounded_facts: Rand declared his enduring love and unwavering faith in Eve's judgment in front of others.
He emphasized that Eve had given him more pleasure than any of his assets and that she had shared his life.

memories.scene_title: 138、INT. RAND MANSION - THIRD FLOOR HALLWAY - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: I held Chauncey’s hand and told him how safe I felt with him, how close—my voice trembled a little, but I needed him to know that Benjamin understands my feelings for him.
grounded_facts: Eve was holding Chance's hand while walking down the hallway.
Eve expressed feeling close and safe with Chance.
Eve mentioned that Benjamin understands and accepts her feelings for Chance.
The dialogue occurred outside her bedroom door.

memories.scene_title: 138、INT. RAND MANSION - THIRD FLOOR HALLWAY - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: I opened my bedroom door and asked him to come in—then closed it behind us, my heart pounding, the air suddenly quieter, just the two of us inside.
grounded_facts: Eve opened her bedroom door and invited Chance to enter.
Chance entered the room.
Eve closed the door after they both went inside.
The action followed immediately after the conversation in the hallway.

memories.memory_text: I kissed him, held him close, ran my hands over his body, but he didn’t respond—just kept watching the TV like I wasn’t even there.
grounded_facts: Eve initiated physical affection by kissing and embracing Chance on the bed.
Chance did not resist or respond to Eve's physical advances.
Chance continued watching television during the embrace.

memories.memory_text: He said he 'likes to watch'—and when I touched myself under the covers, he changed the channel and yawned as I came… then just said 'Good night' and left.
grounded_facts: Chance stated he 'likes to watch' Eve.
Eve self-pleasured while partially covered by the bedsheet, aware of Chance observing.
Chance changed the TV channel and yawned during Eve's orgasm.
Chance left the room after saying 'Good night, Eve.'

memories.memory_text: I stood beside Chance as the President spoke, and then he just turned and walked away into the trees without a word — I remember the cold air, the snow underfoot, and how I stayed still, watching him go.
grounded_facts: Eve was standing with Chance and Allenby during the funeral.
Chance abruptly left the funeral while the President was speaking.
Eve remained at the site and observed Chance walking into the surrounding trees.

memories.memory_text: I said Chauncey was sensitive and overcome with grief when the President asked where he was.
grounded_facts: Eve speaks during a conversation with the President, First Lady, and Allenby after Rand's funeral.
She explains Chauncey's absence by saying he is sensitive and was overcome with grief.
She thanks the First Lady by saying 'Thank you, Nancy' after receiving condolences.

memories.memory_text: I kept looking around, hoping to see Chance, wishing he were there with us—his absence felt heavy as we walked to the limousine.
grounded_facts: Eve expresses a wish that Chance were present at the funeral.
Eve looks around repeatedly while walking to the limousine, searching for Chance.

memories.memory_text: I ran down the hill toward Chauncey, my voice breaking as I called his name, and when I reached him, I held him so tightly—I was trembling, overwhelmed with relief that he was safe.
grounded_facts: Eve calls out to Chance multiple times upon seeing him.
Eve runs down a hill, nearly falling, to reach Chance.
Eve holds Chance tightly upon reaching him, expressing emotional distress and relief.

memories.memory_text: I invited Chance to come home with us so Dr. Allenby could look at his leg—hospitals are so impersonal, and it felt like the right thing to do, even with a stranger in the car.
grounded_facts: Eve suggests Chance see her family doctor instead of going to a hospital
She mentions her husband's doctor and nurses are staying with them
She instructs David to go home and asks Jeffrey to call ahead
She expresses concern about impersonal hospital treatment

memories.memory_text: I offered Chance cognac and introduced myself, but he coughed when he drank it—then he kept searching his pockets for a remote control, which confused me a little.
grounded_facts: Eve offers Chance a drink from the limo’s bar
She pours cognac into a monogrammed glass
She introduces herself as Eve Rand
Chance coughs after sipping
searches pockets for remote control
Eve asks if he lost something

memories.memory_text: I handed my coat to Greta and told Wilson I’d be with Mr. Rand if needed, then said to Chance I’d see him after the doctor checked his leg—he looked around the hallway like he wasn’t sure where he was.
grounded_facts: Eve gave her coat to Greta upon entering the mansion.
Eve instructed Wilson that she would be with Mr. Rand if needed.
Eve addressed Chance formally, referring to him as 'Mr. Gardiner', and mentioned she would see him after the doctor examined his leg.
Eve observed Chance looking around the mansion as Wilson wheeled him away.

memories.memory_text: I held Ben's hand against my cheek and told him Mr. Gardiner was reasonable, but when he joked about meeting him for dinner, I couldn't answer—there was just silence between us.
grounded_facts: Eve entered Benjamin Rand's hospital room and kissed him.
Eve held Benjamin Rand's hand and pressed it against her cheek.
Eve mentioned that Mr. Gardiner was reasonable.
Benjamin Rand suggested inviting Mr. Gardiner to dinner.
Eve did not respond verbally when asked if he was well enough for dinner, resulting in silence.

memories.memory_text: I asked Robert how Mr. Gardiner was. When he suggested Chance stay, I questioned if it was necessary. I said Chance was different, not like people we usually meet in Washington.
grounded_facts: Eve asked Dr. Allenby about Mr. Gardiner's condition.
Dr. Allenby suggested Chance stay at the mansion for a couple of days.
Eve questioned whether staying was necessary.
Eve remarked that Chance was different and not the kind of person one usually meets in Washington.

memories.memory_text: I put my tea down and asked Robert if there had been any improvement in Ben, and when he said no, I just went quiet—staring into the dark, holding onto moments where Ben seemed almost like himself again, even though I know it's not real.
grounded_facts: Eve put down her tea before asking about Ben's condition.
Eve inquired if there was any improvement in Ben.
Dr. Allenby responded with 'No, Eve... I'm sorry.'
Eve expressed internal conflict, recalling fleeting signs of Ben's strength that gave her false hope.
Eve remained silent and looked into the darkness after the exchange.

memories.memory_text: I asked Chauncey about the old man he mentioned, and when he said he was very close to him, I could hear the quiet sadness in his voice—he didn’t say much, but I understood how much he must have meant to him.
grounded_facts: Eve asked Chance about the old man who died.
Chance confirmed he was very close to the old man.
Eve interpreted Chance's brevity and tone as emotional reluctance and depth of feeling.

memories.memory_text: When I asked about Louise and he said she was just the maid who brought him meals, I felt a sudden relief—I had worried there might have been someone else, someone from his past I didn’t know about, but then that weight lifted.
grounded_facts: Eve inquired about Louise after the old man.
Chance clarified Louise was the maid and not romantically involved with him.
Eve expressed internal relief upon learning Louise was not a romantic figure.

memories.memory_text: I told Chauncey that Benjamin and I would be watching him on TV tonight—he seemed pleased by that.
grounded_facts: Eve says, 'We'll both be watching' in reference to viewing Chance on television.
Chance responds, 'That's good,' and smiles.

memories.memory_text: I stepped forward and kissed Chauncey on the cheek—I wasn't planning to, but it felt right in the moment—and then I left, a little flustered.
grounded_facts: Eve impulsively kisses Chance on the cheek after saying 'Good luck, Chauncey.'
After the kiss, Eve turns and leaves the room, slightly embarrassed.

memories.memory_text: I told Chauncey I loved him and wanted us to be very close—so close—and for a moment, I let myself believe he might feel the same.
grounded_facts: Eve confesses her love to Chance and expresses a desire to become emotionally and physically close.
She speaks with difficulty and pauses, indicating emotional vulnerability.

memories.memory_text: I started to cry after I kissed him and he didn’t kiss me back—his arm around me felt like comfort, not love, and I realized my feelings weren’t returned.
grounded_facts: Eve begins to cry after her affection is not reciprocated by Chance.
She kisses him, caresses him, but stops when she realizes he is not responding.
